narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to hear "no" feels like a door slamming shut, and I'm truly sorry it's come to this. Our model looked at your profile and saw a 48% chance of not passing the bar exam, which tipped the scales against admission, but let me walk you through the story it told about you, like piecing together chapters of your journey. The biggest chapter was your Year 3 grades, landing you in the top decile—wait, no, actually in the 10th decile at school, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward challenges ahead, as if that later performance hinted at hurdles in sustaining the grind of bar prep. Right behind it was your Year 1 decile, also a 10.0, reinforcing that early academic footing in law school didn't quite set a firm foundation for the exam's demands. Your LSAT score of 32 wove in next, whispering that while solid, it might not fully equip you for the intense reasoning marathon of the bar. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model saw as limiting access to some of the extra supports that often make the difference. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 added another thread, suggesting undergrad habits might carry over into tougher legal studies. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time studies nudged the picture toward risk, based on patterns the model has learned from thousands of others who've walked this path. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they all intertwined to paint a cautious tale of potential struggle. This isn't the end of your story, though; many have rewritten theirs by targeting those early deciles or bolstering test prep. You've got strengths shining through, and with focus, you can flip this narrative. Hang in there—you're capable of more than any prediction.
